summary: Markets pushed to fresh all-time highs Friday as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq capped their best month since the start of the COVID pandemic in 2020, driven by Apple's earnings beat and renewed Iran peace talk hopes. The rally comes on the heels of Wednesday's dramatic Fed meeting, where Chair Powell's final session saw a historic 8-4 split vote to hold rates steady at 3.5%-3.75%, with four dissents marking the highest since 1992. Powell confirmed he will remain as a Fed governor despite his chairmanship ending May 15, while Kevin Warsh awaits Senate confirmation. Mega-cap tech earnings delivered mixed results: Alphabet surged 7% on a clean beat-and-raise, Apple jumped 3.4% on strong iPhone sales and China performance, while Microsoft and Meta stumbled on AI spending concerns. Oil prices eased on Iran proposal reports, while Bitcoin held above $78,000 as spot ETF inflows reversed three days of outflows.

signals.title: Historic Fed dissent signals policy rift as Warsh confirmation looms
signals.desc: Four FOMC members dissented in the April 29 decision—the most since 1992—with three opposing the easing bias amid elevated inflation from the Iran war, signaling potential tightening under Warsh.

signals.title: Apple earnings beat drives Nasdaq to record close above 25,000
signals.desc: Apple reported Q2 EPS of $2.01 vs. $1.95 est., with strong iPhone sales and China growth, announcing a $100 billion buyback; stock jumped 3.4% and lifted the Nasdaq 0.89% to a new high of 25,114.

signals.title: Bitcoin spot ETF inflows return after three-day selloff
signals.desc: U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded $4.5 million in net inflows on May 1 after nearly $500 million in outflows over three days, as BTC held above $78,000 despite macro uncertainty.

signals.title: Oil surges to 4-year high as Trump vows blockade will stay
signals.desc: Brent crude hit $119.50—highest since June 2022—as President Trump confirmed the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports remains, pressuring airline and energy-sensitive sectors despite diplomatic signals.

signals.title: Mag 7 earnings reveal AI capex scrutiny intensifying
signals.desc: Microsoft fell 5.7% and Meta stumbled on ballooning AI spending plans approaching $645 billion sector-wide in 2026, while Alphabet's monetization clarity drove a 7% rally, splitting investor sentiment on tech.

verdict: Equities and crypto remain resilient at record highs, but Fed policy discord, oil-driven inflation risk, and AI spending fatigue present credible pullback catalysts into next week's jobs data.
